source: FII Institute — Rise of the Gigafirm: next trillion-dollar tech company (panel)
summary: Foundation model companies without a major internet platform, distribution, and proprietary data will see their value asymptote to zero
summary_extended: Baker argues that standalone foundation model companies — those lacking a major internet platform, proprietary data, and distribution infrastructure — will see their value rapidly approach zero as the underlying models become commoditized. His reasoning is that value in the AI stack accrues only to entities with durable data moats and reinforcement learning feedback loops that continuously improve their models using proprietary user-generated data. Without these structural advantages, foundation model capabilities will be replicated or undercut by better-resourced incumbents, leaving pure-play model companies with no defensible position. The investment implication is that large internet platforms with massive proprietary data assets and existing distribution — such as Google, Meta, Microsoft, and Amazon — are the structural winners in the foundation model era, while companies like OpenAI (if ever public) or other standalone AI labs would be structurally disadvantaged. Baker frames this as a logical, near-inevitable consequence of how data moats compound over time, making it a medium-term but high-conviction structural view.
detail: Baker argues that standalone foundation model companies (not attached to a large internet platform with proprietary data and distribution) will be commoditized rapidly. Value accrues only to those with proprietary data moats and reinforcement learning loops.
confidence_evidence: Baker states this directly and frames it as a logical consequence of the shared thesis on data moats
quote_fragment: your value is going to asymptote to zero very quickly

ticker_audit.reasoning: Google is the canonical example of a large internet platform with proprietary data, distribution, and RL feedback loops — the exact profile Baker identifies as the structural winner. Its inclusion is unambiguous from the thesis framing.
Meta has massive proprietary social graph and behavioral data plus global distribution, fitting Baker's description of platforms that will capture AI value versus standalone model companies.
Microsoft has enterprise distribution, proprietary data via Office/LinkedIn, and deep OpenAI integration, making it a clear beneficiary of the platform-plus-data thesis Baker articulates.
Amazon has AWS distribution, proprietary e-commerce and logistics data, and Alexa/Bedrock infrastructure, fitting the platform-with-data-moat profile Baker describes as the structural winner.
